Edge cases like re-checking _is_empty() after the TTL sleep (lines 322-326) and the runner_done callback that ignores superseded tasks (lines 1238-1249) are handled with care.

channel.py _build_and_start_pipeline (lines 1043-1252): ~210-line method that constructs LLMSwitcher, transport, voice switcher, turn detector, pipeline, task, event handlers, plus an inline _setup_pong_responder closure that polls for the data channel. server.py _check_ports_or_exit (lines 403-466) and the two _read_*_seconds helpers (lines 66-146) follow consistent precedence-stair patterns (env → settings → default) with explicit warnings for invalid input — good helper-extraction style.

openclaw.py _process_user_text (lines 511-573) wraps the whole body in try/except, then has another inner try/except around the queue.get() — defensive sprawl: the outer also pushes LLMFullResponseEndFrame on error, while the inner does too, so a queue failure can produce two end frames.
voice_switcher.py _bootstrap_tts_services (lines 57-98): nested loops with two try/except layers and a string-search 'Credentials required' to reclassify a ValueError — fragile error discrimination.

shared/daemon_protocol.py defines length-prefixed JSON helpers (send_message/recv_message) and unix-socket path constants (VOICE_SOCKET_PATH/VOICE_PID_FILE) that server/tts_client.py and server/voice_client.py consume; mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/daemon_bridge.py duplicates both the constants and the wire-protocol code (lines 21-22 reassign the paths, _send_recv at line 104-134 reimplements the length-prefix framing). One seam, two implementations — drift risk if the protocol evolves.

Voice profile switching is reimplemented twice. mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/channel.py:575-621 (VoiceChannel.switch_voice) handles the same-provider/cross-provider TTS switch logic, and server/features/voice_switcher.py:155-303 (VoiceProfileSwitcher._handle_set_voice_profile and switch_profile) implements an almost identical state machine. The channel.py comment near line 1170 acknowledges 'RTVI voice-switcher handlers removed... the VoiceProfileSwitcher instance is still used for its TTS ServiceSwitcher and profile tracking', leaving handle_message + send_server_response code paths unreachable from the production seam.

talky_cli.py duplicates urllib.request.urlopen + urllib.error.HTTPError handling across cmd_profile, cmd_voice, cmd_talkystatus, and others (lines 332-507) — same try/except/json.loads pattern repeated 4+ times. A small `_daemon_get` / `_daemon_post` helper would consolidate the seam between CLI and daemon HTTP API.

Strong examples of well-designed seams elsewhere: VoiceChannel exposes a small surface (warmup/attach/detach/speak/listen/switch_to_profile/switch_voice/request_leave) and the lifecycle table at the top of channel.py documents every entry point. MCPDriverLLMService (mcp_driver_llm_service.py) is a tight, well-documented frame-level adapter: a single ~30-line process_frame routes UserTurnTextFrame to a queue and passes everything else through. Output of listen() is a stable {text, segments[]} dict.

server.py composes a single Starlette app by extending FastMCP's lifespan context (mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/server.py:745-781) — orchestration reads as composition, not entanglement. Webrtc routes, mcp /mcp, and static client are mounted in one builder.

Idle-TTL, request_leave, listen-vs-disconnect race, and pipeline rebuild on disconnect are all expressed as asyncio.Event/asyncio.wait patterns with explicit cancellation in finally blocks (channel.py:418-465, 1003-1019). The d5e6 'flag-based liveness' anti-pattern is explicitly avoided (is_live() reads task state directly, channel.py:243-255).

Profile-greeting / signoff lookup repeats the same shape twice (channel.py:749-787) — both functions build the same try/except wrapper around get_profile_manager().get_llm_backend(name). Minor, but a candidate for one helper.

_check_ports_or_exit (server.py:403-466) duplicates 'lsof -ti :PORT -sTCP:LISTEN' logic that talky_cli.py:38-57 (_kill_pids_on_port) also runs. The port-holder discovery seam between CLI and daemon could share a `shared/network_utils.py` helper (which already exists with related code).

Dual daemons (voice_daemon on unix socket, talky daemon on tcp 9090) are clean as separate concerns, but mcp-server/server.py talks to both; the bridge to the voice daemon (daemon_bridge.py) bypasses shared/daemon_protocol entirely while the talky daemon serves HTTP — slight asymmetry.

mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/server.py: 12 MCP tools all decorated `@mcp.tool()` and uniformly `async def` — consistent shape at the boundary

Return-type drift across MCP tools: `convo_speak -> bool` while siblings (`convo_listen`, `say_local_audio`, `start_convo`, etc.) all return `dict`. One sibling diverges from a strong dict-result convention

Error contracts diverge among similar tools: `switch_profile` and `switch_voice` swallow RuntimeError/ValueError and return `{'error': str(e)}`; `convo_speak`/`convo_listen` let exceptions propagate to the MCP layer; `say_local_audio` etc. delegate to daemon helpers that return `{'success': False, 'error': ...}`. Three different failure shapes for related ops

Result-shape drift: `say_local_audio`/voice daemon helpers use `{'success': bool, ...}`; `switch_profile` uses `{'status': 'ok', 'active': name}` on success and `{'error': str}` on failure (no `success` key, no `status` key on failure). A caller cannot use one predicate across these tools

Voice daemon clients (`server/voice_client.py:send_ask_request`, `server/voice_daemon.py:send_speak_request`) take 5–6 optional params each — at the >5 threshold but parameters are clearly named, defaulted, and homogeneous (all voice-routing options); borderline but acceptable

Two parallel transports exist for speak/listen: `say_local_audio`/`ask_local_audio` (local audio daemon) and `convo_speak`/`convo_listen` (browser pipeline). The verb pairs are intentionally distinct and individually documented, and `talk_local_audio` is an explicit alias for `ask_local_audio` with a docstring explaining why — coherent overall, though it doubles the surface

`request_leave` documentation is precise about its dict shape (`left`, `user_interrupted`, optional `text`), and `join_convo` returns `voice_channel.join_convo()` (status dict). Channel-layer return shapes documented
shared/daemon_protocol.py: small, uniform sync helpers (`send_message`/`recv_message`/`daemon_is_running`); no async/sync mixing here
shared/profile_manager.py public surface is fully sync `get_*`/`list_*` accessors plus a single resolver — consistent shape with no async leak

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: Architecture has a clear intended shape — workspace members server/ and mcp-server/ around a shared/ utilities layer with profile_manager as the config hub — but the boundary between mcp-server (a packaged distributable) and the top-level shared/+server/ tree is enforced by sys.path mutation rather than dependency declaration. The result is a working but fragile arrangement: hub modules concentrate change risk, function-level imports proliferate to dodge cycle issues, and a duplicate services_factory shim with a stale dead-path import sits at the boundary.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: Repeated sys.path manipulation across multiple modules, a one-implementation duplicate factory at the package seam, dense deferred-import patterns around the profile_manager hub, and two unmerged daemons together place this firmly in the 'significant drag' band where developers cannot trust boundaries without reading them. Domain decomposition is sensible and the hub itself is coherent, which keeps it above the severely problematic range, but the boundary debt is systemic rather than isolated.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has a stated architectural narrative (two daemons, in-process voice pipeline, profile-driven LLM switcher) that README and CLAUDE.md do communicate, and within mcp-server/ the file decomposition (channel, daemon_bridge, event_bus, mcp_driver_llm_service, talky_turn) does map to coherent capabilities. But the top-level decomposition is muddled by historical accidents: a 'server/' folder that is consumed by the actual server, two parallel services_factory implementations, a single-file features/ package, a 931-LOC talky_cli.py at root, and persistent legacy/compat shims (tts_daemon, LEGACY_* constants, /api/profile) that the docs even admit are deferred work. A new engineer can read the pipeline diagram but cannot, without ticket-hash archaeology, predict why server/ vs mcp-server/ vs shared/ split the way they do or where new code belongs.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: This sits in the 'mixed' band per global anchors — the project communicates intent through prose docs but the directory shape works against it, with sibling packages whose names invert their roles and a stalled unification explicitly logged in CLAUDE.md. It is above 'significant drag' (60) because the bounded subsystems (server/backends/, mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/) are individually coherent and the README/CLAUDE docs do match runtime reality on the load-bearing pieces. It does not reach 80 because a new engineer would routinely struggle to explain the top-level decomposition, the two-daemon split, or where to put new code — exactly the failure mode the rubric flags.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase is broadly consistent at the surface level — uniform snake_case, a single @dataclass strategy for domain objects, consistent cmd_* CLI handler naming, and shared logger/import patterns across most directories. The drift is concentrated in two structural pockets: the server/backends/ directory hosts three siblings that follow three different protocols and carries an orphan abstraction layer (base.py) plus a stale test that imports nonexistent symbols, and the boundary between shared/service_factory.py and mcp-server/services_factory.py shows naming drift (singular/plural) plus a pass-through wrapper using sys.path manipulation.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: Surface conventions hold up — a developer picking up most files trusts the patterns they've seen elsewhere. But the backends/ style island is real: a developer extending it has to re-learn which of three structural shapes to copy and discover that the documented base classes are dead. That recurring 'which pattern do I follow here?' moment in the most important plugin directory keeps this in the solid-but-uneven 80 band rather than the strong-90 band.

error_consistency.dimension_character: Error handling is mostly intentional and layered: I/O boundaries broadly catch-and-recover (annotated with `# noqa: BLE001`), validation paths raise typed exceptions (ValueError/RuntimeError), and HTTP handlers map them to specific status codes. The notable cracks are missing `from e` chaining at translation boundaries and a small set of swallow-and-log sites in LLM backends, plus a single bare `except:` in logging_config. A developer can usually predict failure behavior, but debugging credential/setup errors loses the original traceback in a way that isn't worth the readability gain.
error_consistency.score_rationale: Pattern adherence is strong enough that a developer would trust the broad-catch sites (they're annotated and recover sensibly) and predict typed raises at validation, placing this above mid-70s. It falls short of solid (80) because exception chaining is inconsistently applied at the most user-visible failure paths and the MCP tool error contract diverges between handlers without a stated rule, both of which produce real friction when investigating failures.

naming_quality.dimension_character: Naming across the codebase is intent-revealing and consistent: snake_case throughout, domain-loaded class names, predicates that honestly return booleans, and factory/loader prefixes used in a uniform way. Generic verbs cluster on framework-mandated boundaries (Pipecat FrameProcessor.process_frame, HTTP route handle_*, Pipecat event on_*) rather than as evasive abstractions, so the apparent 'handle/process/on' counts in the vocabulary scan are not vague names. The few rough edges (two functions sharing the bare name daemon_is_running, one nested smart_filter helper) are local and don't undermine the overall clarity.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Strong, systemic naming discipline with framework-shaped exceptions correctly placed on framework seams puts this above 'solid but uneven' and into the upper band. It is held back from the exemplary range by the duplicated daemon_is_running across two daemon contexts and a small set of mildly generic helper names — minor friction rather than structural problems.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: Most of the codebase favors direct, bounded modules — backends extend Pipecat's LLMService inline, profile_manager exposes typed dataclasses, and the MCPDriverLLMService is a clearly motivated Pipecat extension rather than an invented layer. The drag is a sizeable dead abstraction surface in server/backends/base.py (ABC + manager + mixins with zero callers) plus a few unused pass-through factories (create_pi_service, create_vad_analyzer) that add a hop without policy.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Cleanly direct domain code and only one Dict[str, Any] annotation across the production tree pull the score above the solid-but-uneven anchor at 80. Dead-abstraction debt in backends/base.py and a couple of vestigial factory wrappers prevent a higher score by misleading readers about the real backend interface.

dependency_health.dimension_character: Dependency strategy is mostly thoughtful — a single HTTP/log/config stack, a clean optional-extras taxonomy in the root pyproject, and a well-documented lazy-install pipeline (shared/dependency_installer.py) that defers heavy provider SDKs until a profile actually needs them. However, the mcp-server workspace member contradicts that strategy: it hard-requires kokoro-onnx, requests, and httpx even though they are unused at the talky level, and pins pipecat-ai loosely (>=0.0.101) while the root pins it strictly (==0.0.102), so a developer can't trust a single source of truth for critical versions. The root also declares 'rich' as a runtime dep with no importer.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Score sits in the upper 70s: the codebase has clear deliberate patterns (lazy extras, single-purpose libs, consistent lockfile) but several concrete defects — unused declared deps, a hard-required heavy ML package (kokoro-onnx), and inconsistent version pinning across workspace members — that a developer reviewing dependencies would have to untangle. Not low enough for the 60-band (no pervasive drift), not high enough for 85+ (multiple confirmed defects).

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: Internals split cleanly into two zones: the newer channel/server/CLI core shows deliberate craft (sectioned methods, asyncio races with proper finally cancellation, edge-case re-checks) while the LLM backends and voice_switcher carry older AI-flavored sprawl — near-duplicate v2/v3 function pairs, hasattr/delattr state juggling, repeated put_nowait try/except blocks, and same-provider/cross-provider decision trees implemented two or three times. A developer reading channel.py is well served; a developer extending an LLM backend wades through cargo and copy-paste before reaching the actual protocol.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits between solid-but-uneven (80) and mixed (70). Strengths are real and concentrated in load-bearing files, but the recurring pattern duplication in backends and switcher, combined with one ~210-line pipeline-build method and dead-but-retained v2 auth code, mean a developer working in the older zones must work harder than in the newer zones. Score reflects the unevenness and the multi-file nature of the duplication.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Seams are uneven: the high-traffic paths (VoiceChannel pipeline lifecycle, MCPDriverLLMService frame translation, the composed Starlette+FastMCP+WebRTC lifespan) are well-scoped, well-documented, and read as composition rather than tangle. But several boundaries carry meaningful glue-code entropy — the wire-protocol for the voice daemon is implemented twice, voice-profile switching has two parallel state machines (one of which is partially dead RTVI handler code still wired to the live class), and CLI<->daemon HTTP error handling is copy-pasted across commands. The strong central spine is real, but a developer crossing those particular seams encounters surprising duplication.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase clears the 80 anchor on its hot path (handoffs across the in-process voice pipeline, transport disconnect, profile/voice switch frames, listen/speak races) — these are genuinely well-designed seams with explicit lifecycle and asyncio discipline. It falls short of 85 because of the duplicated daemon-protocol implementation in daemon_bridge.py, the parallel voice-switcher state machines, and the CLI HTTP boilerplate — three concrete, fixable seams that a developer will trip over. None is structural; all are extraction debt.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test portfolio is a small, deliberate sliver of state-machine unit tests on VoiceChannel (TTL, request_leave, greeting) and a credential helper, surrounded by large untested critical modules — the CLI (931 LOC), the unified daemon entry (846 LOC), the in-process voice channel build/switch paths, the voice daemon, the LLM backends, and the 19-importer profile manager all run with no direct verification. Where tests exist they are well-scoped and explicit about what they don't cover, but a stale test_config.py imports a non-existent module path and several `test_*.py` files in server/tests and mcp-server/ are print-driven scripts with no asserts, masquerading as tests.
test_strategy.score_rationale: A developer changing talky_cli.py, server.py, channel.py outside the three covered helpers, profile_manager, the voice daemon, or any backend cannot trust that a test suite will catch regressions — that is the definition of significant drag and pulls the score below 40. The good unit-test craft on the covered slices and the absence of fragility patterns prevents a deeper drop into the 20s.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The public surfaces are mostly orderly: MCP tools share a uniform `@mcp.tool() async def` shape, the channel class has a clear async-for-IO / sync-for-query split, and shared protocol helpers are tight. The friction is concentrated in the result envelopes — sibling tools return `dict`, `bool`, `{success,...}`, `{status,...}`, or raise — so a developer cannot reuse one error/success predicate across the MCP surface and must read each tool to know what to expect.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: Strong shape consistency at the call-site level (decorator, async, naming, parameter ordering) keeps this above 70, but the recurring success/error envelope drift and one bool-returning outlier among dict-returning siblings is exactly the 'recurring irregularity' that the 80 anchor describes. Landing at 76 reflects a solid surface with a single but pervasive coherence gap that a developer will hit repeatedly when error-handling MCP tool calls.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is notably free of the most damaging LLM-debt smells: comments explain rationale and reference tickets, defensive checks sit at real I/O boundaries, and there is no restating-comment or nosy-logging epidemic. The remaining noise is concentrated in two places — the moltis/openclaw backends, which were clearly cloned from the same template, and a handful of tiny pass-through helpers — making the surface uneven rather than systemically slop-laden.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The dominant signal is intentional, hand-edited code with clear WHY-oriented comments, which would normally support a strong (90+) score. The two backend modules' near-duplication, an emoji-heavy log style, and minor wrappers/redundant imports pull it into the solid-but-uneven band around 84 — a developer would mostly trust the code but hit obvious boilerplate twins when working on backends.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: Migration discipline is broadly present — transitions are tied to ticket numbers, intent is documented in module docstrings, and there's no JS/TS or class/functional split. But the codebase carries several genuinely stale shims (a backward-compat tts_daemon wrapper with no callers, a module-level profile_manager singleton with no callers, a legacy_daemon_is_running with no callers) plus a real coexistence: two LLM-aggregator patterns live in parallel because the voice_daemon path was deferred, and the 'legacy' /api/profile route is in fact the only route the current CLI uses. The labels lie in places — what is marked 'legacy' is sometimes the active contract and what is marked 'backwards compat' is sometimes orphaned.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: Mid-range score: the codebase isn't a migration graveyard, but it isn't tidy either — multiple identifiable shims have outlived their callers, and at least one structural coexistence (two aggregator patterns, two TalkyProfile backend fields) reflects a paused migration rather than an active one. Below the 75-band because evidence of orphan compat code is direct and unambiguous; above the 50-band because most callers have moved to the new shapes and migrations are at least named and tracked.

package_organization.dimension_character: The high-level layering is clean and intentional: shared/ is a real utility hub with one-way fan-in from every consumer, mcp-server/ owns the daemon, server/ owns audio backends, and root entry points are entry points. The friction is at the second level — server/ root collects unrelated audio-pipeline concerns into one flat namespace, and server/features/ is a one-occupant folder that anticipates structure that does not yet exist.
package_organization.score_rationale: Clear directional coupling, justified root files, and a coherent shared/ keep this comfortably above the solid-but-uneven anchor. It is held back from higher by the flat server/ root and the thin features/ folder, both of which a developer would have to read individually rather than navigate by directory.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: Initialization is a mix of disciplined patterns (a real lazy `get_profile_manager()` getter, deferred warmup via Starlette lifespan in channel.py) and stubborn import-time side effects that undermine them. The bare `profile_manager = get_profile_manager()` at module scope plus the orphan sys.path inserts and the eager module-level VoiceChannel/FastMCP construction in server.py mean importing any of these modules triggers filesystem I/O, env writes, and config loads — fragile in tests and noisy in tools that just want a small symbol. The codebase shows awareness of the problem (getter exists, lifespan warmup exists) but hasn't followed through on removing the redundant eager snapshots.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Solid getter discipline at the call sites and a reasonable lifespan-based warmup model push this above 'significant drag'. The import-time YAML load in profile_manager.py:475, the broad sys.path mutation pattern, the dead `config.profile_manager` import in services_factory.py, and the heavy module-import side effects in server.py keep it well below 'solid but uneven' — the issues are systemic across the boot path rather than isolated.

design_coherence.dimension_character: Most modules show clear separation of concerns — the MCP tool layer in server.py is a thin facade over VoiceChannel, daemon_bridge and event_bus are tight single-purpose units, and large files like channel.py earn their length through genuine domain complexity rather than mixed concerns. The drag comes from a small number of repeated structural patterns that should be data-driven (profile_manager's triple-branch backend constructor, server.py's two near-identical settings readers, talky_cli's cmd_pi/cmd_claude twins) and from two long functions (_build_and_start_pipeline, _do_listen) that fold five-plus distinct steps into a single body.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Solid-but-uneven territory: the architectural shape is coherent and the patterns that are followed are followed consistently, but a developer would repeatedly hit copy-paste branches in the merge/fallback code and would have to scroll through 200-line orchestration functions to understand pipeline construction. The defects are concentrated rather than systemic, which keeps this above the 70 line; the duplication in profile_manager is structural enough to keep it well below 85.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The mcp-server channel/server layer is exemplary on contracts — return types are honest, docstrings describe raise conditions and edge cases (e.g. disconnect during listen, grace-window semantics in request_leave), and tool wrappers match underlying behavior. The shared/ and server/backends/ layers are weaker: a handful of `param: str = None` annotation lies, a `-> str` stub that always raises NotImplementedError, a 'shutdown' helper that returns before its work completes, and a few public factory helpers missing return annotations.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Strong patterns dominate the conversation/orchestration core, which is the most contract-sensitive surface, but the integration edges (auth/token loaders, Pi stub, http session shutdown, service factories) carry recurring small lies a developer would hit when wiring backends. That mix maps to the 80–85 'solid but uneven' band — recurring local friction, no systemic contract collapse.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Control flow is overwhelmingly direct: AST sweeps for the canonical clarity smells (identical branches, dead code, boolean simplifications, constant conditions) found nothing across the production tree. The one consistent blemish is a cluster of async-by-decoration methods in shared/client_launcher.py (and one in openclaw.py) that do blocking work and never await — a localized concurrency fib rather than a control-flow tangle.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Solid-to-strong on the 80-anchor: structural clarity is intentional and the codebase reads top-to-bottom with meaningful branches. The async-not-await cluster keeps it from a stronger score because it makes the runtime model harder to reason about at the launcher seam, but the issue is bounded to one module and trivially fixable.

type_safety.dimension_character: Type annotations are present on most public APIs, dataclasses are honest about Optional fields, and `type: ignore` comments are confined to test mocks with specific error codes. The main weakness is a single Optional/required mismatch where TalkyProfile's Optional[str] fields are fed into accessors that promise `name: str`, plus a few looser surfaces (bare `dict`, untyped CLI handler args) that don't actively mislead but leave honesty on the table.
type_safety.score_rationale: Solid type discipline overall — a developer can mostly trust the annotations as documentation. The Optional flow-through bug in profile resolution is real but isolated, and the under-annotated CLI/daemon-protocol surfaces are conventional rather than misleading. Lands above 80 (mostly trustworthy with isolated soft spots) but short of 90 because the resolve_talky_profile path is the kind of mismatch the rubric explicitly calls out.

evidence: directory_profiles for server/features/ reports file_count 2 (one __init__.py + voice_switcher.py), total_loc 327.
Imported by mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/ and the worktree only — no sibling 'features' to justify the folder.

issues.summary: Sysadmin shell scripts split across /scripts/ and /server/scripts/ without a clear ownership rule
issues.suggestion: Consolidate into one /scripts/ at the workspace root: `git mv server/scripts/kill_opencv.sh scripts/kill-opencv.sh && git mv server/scripts/README.md scripts/README.md && rmdir server/scripts`. Normalize naming to hyphenated to match the existing /scripts/ files. No code import updates needed.
evidence: /scripts/ holds generate-certs.sh and kill-talky.sh (process and certs at workspace level).
/server/scripts/ holds kill_opencv.sh — the only script and a README, despite being inside server/ which is otherwise a Python package.
Hyphen vs underscore mismatch (kill-talky.sh vs kill_opencv.sh) reinforces that the two directories were not designed together.

issues.summary: Module-level `profile_manager = get_profile_manager()` triggers full config bootstrap on import
issues.suggestion: Delete the line `profile_manager = get_profile_manager()` at shared/profile_manager.py:475 (and the 'Backwards compat' comment block). All consumers already use the getter. This removes filesystem I/O and YAML parsing from the import path, dramatically improving testability and reducing fragility when ~/.talky is unwritable.
evidence: shared/profile_manager.py:475 — `profile_manager = get_profile_manager()` runs at import time, calling __init__ which mkdirs ~/.talky, copies bundled default YAML files, and reads 5 YAML configs.

All consumers across server/, mcp-server/, talky_cli.py use `from shared.profile_manager import get_profile_manager` — none reference the bare `profile_manager` attribute. The eager assignment is dead 'backwards compat'.

issues.summary: services_factory.py imports nonexistent `config.profile_manager`; function is unused
issues.suggestion: Either delete `create_services_from_profile()` (dead) or fix the import to `from shared.profile_manager import get_profile_manager` and drop the redundant sys.path insert. Preferred: delete — it duplicates logic already in channel.py's pipeline build.
evidence: Line 84-85: `sys.path.insert(0, ...server)` then `from config.profile_manager import get_profile_manager`. server/config has no profile_manager module — only voice_prompts.py and yaml subdirs.
`create_services_from_profile()` has no callers anywhere in the repo (grep confirms). It would crash with ImportError if invoked.

issues.summary: Endemic sys.path.insert at module scope across CLI, daemon, mcp-server
issues.suggestion: Make `shared/` and `server/` proper installable packages (they already have __init__.py) and rely on `uv tool install --editable .` to put them on sys.path via the workspace. Drop every module-level sys.path.insert. Where genuinely needed (e.g. test bootstrap), centralize in conftest.py rather than repeating in each module.
evidence: talky_cli.py:24-25 inserts project root + server/ unconditionally at import.
channel.py:85-89 walks three `..` levels up from __file__ to compute talky_root and inserts it; comment acknowledges the layout fragility.

services_factory.py:16 inserts `shared/` then imports `service_factory` (without the `shared.` prefix), creating two ways to import the same module — `shared.service_factory` and `service_factory` — which can produce duplicate module objects with separate `_http_sessions` caches.
server/voice_daemon.py:26, say_command.py:11, tts_daemon.py:12, tts_client.py:13 all do `sys.path.insert(0, dirname(dirname(abspath(__file__))))` — identical incantation copy-pasted.

issues.summary: Importing pipecat_mcp_server.server constructs FastMCP, reads YAML, builds VoiceChannel
issues.suggestion: Move FastMCP/VoiceChannel construction and the TTL/grace reads into a `build_app()` factory called from `if __name__ == '__main__'` and the lifespan startup hook. Tools, scripts, or tests that just want to import a tool definition should not pay for ProfileManager bootstrap and VoiceChannel allocation.
evidence: Lines 53,57-59: `logger.add(...)`, `mcp = FastMCP(...)` at module scope — env reads + server object creation on import.

Lines 105 and 149: `_idle_ttl_seconds = _read_idle_ttl_seconds()` and `_request_leave_grace_seconds = _read_request_leave_grace_seconds()` at module scope. Both transitively call `get_profile_manager()`, triggering full config bootstrap during module import.
Lines 154-157: `voice_channel = VoiceChannel(...)` constructed eagerly at module scope before the Starlette lifespan even exists.

issues.summary: Module-level `_http_sessions` dict keeps aiohttp ClientSession refs across event loops
issues.suggestion: Move the session cache onto the VoiceChannel / app state object (or a context manager owned by the lifespan), and drop the module-level dict. Take a session as a constructor argument where used. Eliminates loop-binding surprises and the dual-import duplicate-cache failure mode.
evidence: shared/service_factory.py:14 `_http_sessions: Dict[str, Any] = {}` — populated lazily via `get_http_session()` and never tied to an app lifespan.

shared/service_factory.py:33-49 `close_http_sessions()` falls back to `asyncio.run(...)` when no loop is running. If two event loops run in the same process (e.g. test harness + daemon), sessions bound to one loop will fail when used from the other.

Combined with the syspath hack in services_factory.py:16 (which imports `service_factory` without the `shared.` prefix), the same module can be imported under two names with two separate `_http_sessions` dicts.

issues.summary: cmd_pi and cmd_claude in talky_cli.py are byte-identical apart from the profile string
issues.suggestion: Replace both with a single factory: `def _make_client_profile_cmd(profile_name): def cmd(args): args_dict = {k: v for k, v in vars(args).items() if k != 'func'}; args.profile = profile_name; return cmd_run_client_profile(args); return cmd` and use `pi_parser.set_defaults(func=_make_client_profile_cmd('pi'))`. The local Args class is unnecessary — assigning `args.profile` on the existing Namespace is sufficient since cmd_run_client_profile reads it via getattr.
evidence: talky_cli.py:782-794 cmd_pi defines a local Args class, copies vars(args), constructs Args('pi', ...) and calls cmd_run_client_profile.
talky_cli.py:802-814 cmd_claude does the same with 'claude'.

issues.summary: ProfileManager._load_llm_backends repeats the LLMBackend(...) constructor block in three branches
issues.suggestion: Refactor to a two-stage merge: (1) build a merged dict by `core | defaults_only_for_new_keys | user_extensions` using deep-merge helpers, (2) materialise into `self.llm_backends` with a single LLMBackend(**config) loop. Wrap the user-extension YAML read in a try/except returning {} on FileNotFoundError or unexpected errors so the materialise step has one path. Same shape for voice backends.
evidence: shared/profile_manager.py:117-205 the user-override path (149-160), the no-user-extensions path (172-181), the FileNotFoundError fallback (185-193), and the broad-Exception fallback (197-205) each rebuild LLMBackend with the same six-field construction.
_load_voice_backends:207-262 has a parallel triple-branch merge structure for the (backend_type, provider_name) two-level dict.

issues.summary: server.py defines two near-identical env-var-then-YAML setting readers
issues.suggestion: Extract a single helper `def _read_room_setting(env_key: str, yaml_key: str, *, default: float | None, allow_infinity: bool, clamp_floor: float | None) -> float | None`. Both call sites collapse to one-line wrappers. This both removes duplication and centralises the env-vs-yaml precedence rule so future settings inherit it.
evidence: _read_idle_ttl_seconds:66-102 reads TALKY_ROOM_IDLE_TTL_SECS, falls back to settings['room']['idle_ttl_seconds'], parses float-or-infinity, validates positive.
_read_request_leave_grace_seconds:108-146 mirrors the same env→yaml→default ladder with clamp-to-zero instead of infinity handling.

issues.summary: _build_and_start_pipeline mixes pipeline construction, pong-responder setup, lifecycle handlers, and runner kickoff
issues.suggestion: Split into private helpers on VoiceChannel: `_build_llm_switcher(pm) -> (LLMSwitcher, profile_map)`, `_build_voice_pipeline(stt, llm_switcher, voice_switcher, transport, turn_detector) -> (Pipeline, PipelineTask)`, `_attach_lifecycle_handlers(transport, pipeline_task)`, `_attach_pong_responder(connection)`. Keep _build_and_start_pipeline as the orchestrator that calls these in order and commits state. Don't add new types — just method extraction.
evidence: channel.py:1043-1252 in one function: instantiates STT, builds the LLM switcher map (with per-backend try/except), spawns a `_setup_pong_responder` background task that polls for the data channel, builds the transport, constructs VoiceProfileSwitcher and sets the task back on it, builds the turn detector, builds the Pipeline and PipelineTask, registers transport on_client_connected/on_client_disconnected handlers, installs an upstream frame filter and on_user_speaking handler, commits state to self.*, starts the runner task, and registers _on_runner_done.
Each of those is a coherent step but they accumulate to a 200-line body with five distinct concerns.

issues.summary: voice_daemon._do_listen folds STT setup, pipeline build, two timeout strategies, and result packaging into one function
issues.suggestion: Extract `_build_listen_pipeline(stt) -> (PipelineTask, result_holder)` and `_run_with_timeouts(task, silence_timeout, listen_timeout, speech_started_flag) -> bool` (returns True if hard-cap tripped). _do_listen becomes: build, play start tone, run-with-timeouts, play stop tone, package result. The two timeouts are independent strategies and reading them as separate helpers makes the contract obvious.
evidence: server/voice_daemon.py:230-386 builds STT, plays start tone, builds LocalAudioTransport, constructs LLMContextAggregatorPair with VAD + UserTurnStrategies, registers on_user_turn_started and on_user_turn_stopped handlers, runs a silence-monitor coroutine, applies an asyncio.wait_for hard-cap, plays stop tone, and returns one of three result-dict shapes.
result_holder dict pattern signals the function is coordinating async events that would naturally live on a small helper class or be returned via a queue.

issues.reasoning: event_bus.py is 64 lines, single-purpose. The two suppressed-error sites are intentional: QueueFull pass (drop slow consumers) and ValueError on subscribers.remove (already-removed). Both are documented behaviours, not design problems.

server.py's sys.exit() inside _check_ports_or_exit is appropriate — it runs at process startup before any framework is up; raising would bubble into uvicorn import code with no handler. The function name is honest about exiting.
Worktree spike file (.claude/worktrees/spike+livekit-explore/...) — not part of the production tree, intentionally exploratory.
Worktree spike file — out of production scope.
Worktree spike file — out of production scope.

issues.summary: openclaw.py functions declare `param: str = None` instead of Optional[str]
issues.suggestion: Change the annotations to Optional[str] (or `str | None`) on these three signatures so the type matches reality.
evidence: load_paired_tokens(openclaw_dir: str = None) at line 40 — default None contradicts annotation
load_device_identity(openclaw_dir: str = None) at line 86 — same pattern

OpenClawLLMService.__init__(*, gateway_url: str = None, agent_id: str = 'main', session_key: str = None, **kwargs) at line 250 — gateway_url and session_key both have None defaults under str annotations

issues.summary: PiLLMService.send_message annotated `-> str` but always raises NotImplementedError
issues.suggestion: Either remove the method (Pi backend has its own UI, no caller path) or annotate it `-> typing.NoReturn` and document that this backend deliberately does not support direct injection.
evidence: Line 111: `async def send_message(self, message: str) -> str:` body is exclusively `raise NotImplementedError(...)`
Class is named PiLLMService but does not subclass pipecat's LLMService, so the method exists only to satisfy a duck-type that it cannot actually satisfy.

issues.summary: close_http_sessions() returns before sessions actually close when called from a running loop
issues.suggestion: Make it `async def close_http_sessions()` and have callers await it. If a sync shim is needed for non-async shutdown paths, keep it but document that it is best-effort and only blocks when no loop is running.
evidence: Line 33–49: docstring says 'Call this during application shutdown', but the running-loop branch does `loop.create_task(_close_sessions())` and returns immediately, so the caller has no way to know when sessions are actually closed.
The non-loop branch uses asyncio.run and does block — behavior diverges by call context, which the docstring does not warn about.

issues.summary: Public service factory helpers lack return annotations despite being part of a typed module
issues.suggestion: Annotate the factories with their concrete return types (`-> Any` is acceptable for the dynamic-import factories; `-> SileroVADAnalyzer` for create_vad_analyzer; Optional[str] params on create_tts_for_profile).
evidence: shared/service_factory.py:74 _create_service_from_backend_config(...) — no return annotation
shared/service_factory.py:112 create_stt_service_from_config(provider: str, **kwargs) — no return annotation
shared/service_factory.py:132 create_tts_service_from_config(provider: str, **kwargs) — no return annotation
shared/voice_config.py:16 create_tts_for_profile(voice_profile_name=None, provider=None, voice_id=None) — no parameter or return annotations
shared/voice_config.py:44 create_vad_analyzer() — no return annotation

issues.summary: switch_profile / switch_voice MCP tools document `-> dict` but bury errors as {'error': str} silently
issues.suggestion: Either let the exception propagate (FastMCP serializes it) for consistency with the other tools, or document the {'error': str} branch explicitly in the Returns section so callers know to check for it.
evidence: Lines 341–355 and 367–383: catch (RuntimeError, ValueError) and return {'error': str(e)}, but the docstring's Returns section says 'Dict with status and active profile name' — error shape is undocumented.
Other tools (start_convo, list_profiles) consistently include 'success' or just raise; this is the outlier.

issues.summary: Multiple `async def` methods in shared/client_launcher.py never await anything and use blocking subprocess/sleep calls
issues.suggestion: Either drop the `async` keyword on these methods (and update the two callers in talky_cli.py and _launch_claude to call them synchronously), or replace the blocking subprocess.run/time.sleep calls with `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` and `asyncio.sleep` so the coroutine actually cooperates. Pick one — the current shape promises concurrency the implementation cannot deliver.
evidence: _launch_pi (line 30) is async but only calls subprocess.run/Popen.
trigger_voice_command (line 141) uses time.sleep + webbrowser.open with no awaits.
stop_all (line 161) iterates self.processes synchronously; stop (line 224) is a one-line log.

DaemonManager.ensure_running (line 187) is async but every call inside is blocking subprocess.run / time.sleep — it is awaited from talky_cli.py:891 and _launch_claude under the impression that it yields, but it does not.

issues.summary: OpenClawLLMService._clear_pending_responses is async but performs only synchronous Queue.get_nowait/delattr
issues.suggestion: Drop `async` from `_clear_pending_responses` and update callers (or, if the function is expected to grow into something that performs awaits, leave a comment locking in the contract). Today it is a sync helper masquerading as a coroutine.
evidence: Lines 295-305: `async def _clear_pending_responses(self)` drains a Queue with get_nowait inside a while loop — no await, no I/O.
Callers must `await` this to get clearing semantics; making it async hints at suspension points that do not exist.

issues.summary: resolve_talky_profile passes Optional[str] fields into accessors typed as `name: str`
issues.suggestion: Either tighten the accessor signatures to accept Optional[str] and return Optional[...] (already do), or guard explicitly in resolve_talky_profile: raise ValueError if tp.llm_backend is None / tp.voice_profile is None before dispatch. Cleanest: split TalkyProfile into a raw config dataclass and a resolved one where llm_backend/voice_profile are required str.
evidence: TalkyProfile.llm_backend and .voice_profile are typed Optional[str] = None (lines 54–55).

resolve_talky_profile (line 442) calls self.get_llm_backend(tp.llm_backend) and self.get_voice_profile(tp.voice_profile) — both accessors are annotated `name: str` (lines 415, 418), so a None value silently violates the signature and only produces ValueError because dict.get(None) returns None.
Loaders set llm_backend / voice_profile via `config.get(...) or self.defaults.get(...)` (lines 323–324, 346–347), which can resolve to None when both are missing.

issues.summary: send_message/recv_message use bare `dict` instead of dict[str, Any]
issues.suggestion: Annotate as `dict[str, Any]` (or define a `Mapping[str, Any]`/TypedDict if the message envelope is stable, e.g. `{'type': str, 'payload': ...}`).
evidence: Line 22: `def send_message(sock: socket.socket, data: dict) -> None` — `data` is annotated as bare `dict` with no value type.
Line 28: `def recv_message(sock: socket.socket, timeout: float = 30.0) -> dict` — same.
These functions are the wire protocol surface used by both voice_daemon.py and clients; loose typing here propagates Any through every call site.

issues.summary: AsyncGenerator imported in server/backends/base.py but never used
issues.suggestion: Remove AsyncGenerator from the import list.
evidence: Line 9: `from typing import Any, AsyncGenerator, Dict, Optional` — AsyncGenerator has no references in the file.

add.header: shared/ reached via sys.path, not declared dep
add.description: mcp-server and talky_cli rely on runtime sys.path injection to import shared/ and server/, because shared/ is not a workspace member. This is the structural source of multiple boundary defects (duplicate factories, deferred imports, broken legacy paths).

add.header: profile_manager is the config hub (19 importers)
add.description: shared/profile_manager.py is the dominant import target across the codebase. Its hub role is intentional and the API is coherent, but it concentrates change blast radius and is reached through 11 deferred function-level imports in mcp-server.

add.header: Domain-aligned package decomposition
add.description: Top-level packages (server/ for backends+local-audio, mcp-server/ for MCP+WebRTC daemon, shared/ for cross-cutting config) map cleanly to capabilities. The intended architecture is sensible — what undermines it is the boundary enforcement, not the partition.

add.header: Two parallel daemons coexist
add.description: voice_daemon.py (unix socket, local audio) and the talky/mcp daemon (tcp:9090, WebRTC) run as siblings with separate lifecycles and protocols. CLAUDE.md flags the consolidation as deferred; daemon_protocol.py still carries LEGACY_* constants.

add.header: Two-daemon split is intentional but unfinished
add.description: voice_daemon (local audio over unix socket) and talky daemon (mcp-server, port 9090) are split for runtime reasons, but CLAUDE.md explicitly defers unification. The split drives most top-level decomposition friction: parallel services_factory files, an inverted server/ package name, and legacy LEGACY_* constants in shared/daemon_protocol.py.

add.header: Documented pipeline shape matches runtime
add.description: README and CLAUDE.md accurately describe the Mic→VAD→STT→LLMSwitcher→TTS→Speaker pipeline, the in-process WebRTC handler, the MCPDriverLLMService null passthrough, and ManuallySwitchServiceFrame-based profile switching. The load-bearing architectural claims hold up against the code.

add.header: server/backends/ is a clean domain package
add.description: server/backends/ holds base.py + one file per LLM provider (moltis, openclaw, pi) and is consistently a Pipecat LLMService subclass. Sibling decomposition is uniform and aligns with the documented extension point.

add.header: shared/ is a util grab-bag, not a domain package
add.description: shared/ holds eight unrelated cross-cutting modules (profile_manager, service_factory, daemon_protocol, voice_config, dependency_installer, client_launcher, network_utils, audio_cues) with avg fan-in 6.8. It functions as the project's util layer and is the natural target for any code that doesn't fit elsewhere — high pull, weak ownership.

add.header: Ticket-hash narrative in architecture docs
add.description: CLAUDE.md leans on ticket hashes (58db, ea77, c3a1, 5098, 9d02, 8c9d, 3769fe4) as load-bearing context. New engineers cannot decode the decomposition without git archaeology, even though the prose is otherwise accurate.

add.header: Uniform snake_case across all production modules
add.description: Every directory (server/, mcp-server/, shared/, server/backends/) uses snake_case for module and function names. Top-level CLI handlers all follow cmd_* naming. A developer can rely on the naming pattern they've seen.

add.header: Single domain-object strategy: @dataclass only
add.description: Domain types (LLMBackend, VoiceProfile, TalkyProfile, talky_turn types, event_bus events) all use @dataclass. No TypedDict/NamedTuple drift exists — the 'mixed type strategies' look_for has no signal here.

add.header: server/backends/ is a structural style island
add.description: The directory hosts three siblings (Moltis, OpenClaw, Pi) that follow three different protocols and a base.py file with a fully-built but unused parallel abstraction (BaseLLMBackend, mixins, BackendManager singleton). A stale test imports symbols that don't exist. This is the main convention-drift pocket in the codebase.

add.header: Duplicate factory boundary between shared and mcp-server
add.description: shared/service_factory.py owns the real logic; mcp-server/.../services_factory.py is a pass-through wrapper using sys.path.insert plus a singular/plural naming drift. A developer searching for 'where do services get built' lands twice.

add.header: Worktree mirrors are not production
add.description: .claude/worktrees/spike+livekit-explore/ contains mirrored copies of server/, shared/, and mcp-server/ — every duplicate_clusters finding points there. Not evidence of production drift.

add.header: Annotated broad excepts at I/O boundaries
add.description: WebRTC handlers, lifespan hooks, and the voice channel use `except Exception ... # noqa: BLE001` with logger.error/warning plus recovery. The annotation indicates the broad catch is deliberate, not accidental — these are top-level boundary handlers where crash-on-failure would tear down the whole daemon.

add.header: Layered error strategy by module purpose
add.description: Validation/factory modules (services_factory, daemon_protocol, voice_config) raise typed exceptions; runtime/orchestration modules (channel, voice_daemon, backends) catch and recover. This is the rubric's 'different strategies for different layers' pattern, applied consistently.

add.header: HTTP handlers map exceptions to specific status codes
add.description: handle_switch_profile/handle_switch_voice catch RuntimeError -> 409 and ValueError -> 404 separately, giving callers semantic distinction between 'wrong state' and 'unknown name'. Worth preserving.

add.header: Missing `from e` exception chaining
add.description: services_factory and openclaw re-raise inside except without `from e`, losing original traceback at exactly the boundaries (credential setup, backend connection) where users debug failures. Cheap fix, real signal-to-noise improvement.

add.header: Tool error contract is split without a documented rule
add.description: switch_profile/switch_voice return `{"error": ...}` dicts; convo_speak/convo_listen propagate RuntimeError. No comment explains which shape to use for which tool — picking one rule (or documenting the split) would help future tool authors stay consistent.

add.header: Generic verbs sit on framework seams, not in app code
add.description: process_frame, handle_*, on_* appear as Pipecat FrameProcessor overrides, aiohttp route handlers, and Pipecat event callbacks. The naming_vocabulary aggregate (handle:22, on:14, process:7) reflects framework conformance, not vague application naming — these are not candidates for renaming.

add.header: Predicates honestly typed and prefixed
add.description: is_/_is_ predicates (is_initialized, is_live, _is_empty, _is_tool_env, daemon_is_running) all return bool. No getX-that-mutates patterns observed across server/, shared/, mcp-server/src/.

add.header: Factory/loader/list prefix discipline
add.description: create_*, load_*, list_*, get_*, ensure_* are used consistently for their conventional roles (factory, deserializer, enumeration, accessor, idempotent setup). create_services_from_profile, load_paired_tokens, list_voice_profiles, ensure_daemon, ensure_dependencies are representative.

add.header: Domain-loaded class names
add.description: Class names encode subsystem and role: VoiceChannel, MCPDriverLLMService, TalkyUserTurnDetector, ProfileManager, BackendManager, VoiceProfileSwitcher, AppLauncher, DaemonManager. Readers can infer responsibility without opening the file.

add.header: Two-daemon naming overlap
add.description: Voice daemon and talky daemon each expose a daemon_is_running predicate under the same bare name in different modules. Module path disambiguates at import time but bare-name greps and reader memory can collide. Minor friction tied to the deferred daemon-unification work.

add.header: Backends extend Pipecat LLMService directly
add.description: MoltisLLMService and OpenClawLLMService inherit from pipecat.services.llm_service.LLMService rather than a project-local ABC. This keeps the real backend contract aligned with the framework and removes a wrapper layer.

add.header: Typed dataclasses around YAML config
add.description: profile_manager exposes LLMBackend / VoiceProfile / TalkyProfile dataclasses while keeping nested provider-specific config as Dict[str, Any]. This bounds the typed surface to what the daemon needs without inventing schemas for every provider.

add.header: Dead BaseLLMBackend abstraction surface
add.description: server/backends/base.py defines an ABC, two mixins, and a manager singleton with zero concrete users. The file is misleading documentation about how to add a backend.

add.header: Speculative one-line factory wrappers
add.description: create_pi_service and create_vad_analyzer add an indirection hop without encoding any policy. They are remnants of a symmetry-driven factory pattern that the rest of the codebase abandoned.

add.header: MCPDriverLLMService is a deliberate Pipecat extension
add.description: The null/passthrough LLM service is documented as an LLMSwitcher peer with explicit frame-handling rules — framework extension, not invented indirection. Skip when scoring.

add.header: Lazy provider extras with PROVIDER_TO_EXTRA map
add.description: shared/dependency_installer.py defers heavy TTS/STT provider SDKs until a profile activates by mapping provider names to root-pyproject extras and shelling out to `uv tool install --reinstall --with`. This keeps the baseline install small and is documented in pyproject comments.

add.header: Coherent extras taxonomy in root pyproject
add.description: Optional dependencies are organized as per-provider (tts-*/stt-*), per-cloud (aws, azure, groq, fal), grouped (tts, stt, audio), and convenience (all). No duplicate libraries serve the same purpose at the talky level — single logger (loguru), single config format (yaml), single UI lib (InquirerPy).

add.header: Workspace pin drift between root and mcp-server
add.description: Root pins pipecat-ai==0.0.102 but mcp-server pins >=0.0.101 with a different (larger) extras set. The two venvs resolve independently per CLAUDE.md, so this is a real divergence risk despite the shared lockfile.

add.header: mcp-server hard-requires several unused-at-talky-level deps
add.description: kokoro-onnx, requests, and httpx are declared as runtime deps of mcp-server but no talky source file imports them. kokoro-onnx in particular duplicates the lazy-install path for the kokoro TTS provider.

add.header: Channel internals favor explicit asyncio races over flags
add.description: VoiceChannel.listen and request_leave use asyncio.wait against speech/disconnect/onset events with finally-cancelled pending tasks, instead of relying on a _started boolean. The d5e6 reconnect-zombie post-mortem is referenced in code comments — the pattern is deliberate and pays off in clarity at the call sites.

add.header: Sectioned method headers in long classes
add.description: channel.py uses ── attach / detach ──, ── speak / listen ── style banner comments to chunk a 1250-line module into navigable phases. A developer can scan for the section they need rather than reading top-to-bottom.

add.header: Older backends carry hasattr/delattr accumulators
add.description: openclaw and moltis manage streaming response state via hasattr(self, '_accumulated_response') + delattr instead of __init__-declared attrs. Reads as cryptic state mutation and forces three repeated put_nowait/QueueFull blocks per file.

add.header: Switching logic implemented three times
add.description: Same-provider vs cross-provider TTS switch lives in voice_switcher._handle_set_voice_profile, voice_switcher.switch_profile, and channel.switch_voice — three near-identical decision trees with subtly different error reporting. Indicates a missing single 'apply_profile' method on VoiceProfileSwitcher.

add.header: Dead v2 auth path retained alongside v3
add.description: openclaw.build_device_auth (v2) is unused — only build_device_auth_v3 is called from _connect. Both share a verbatim SPKI-prefix-stripping block with magic 44-byte length checks. Reading time spent on dead code.

add.header: Composed lifespan in unified Starlette app
add.description: server.py wraps FastMCP's existing lifespan_context with a _composed_lifespan that runs voice_channel.warmup, health polling, and ready-file write inside uvicorn's event loop. Composition rather than reinvention — the seam between MCP, WebRTC handler, and static client is one builder.

add.header: VoiceChannel exposes a small, lifecycle-clear surface
add.description: warmup / attach / detach / speak / listen / switch_to_profile / switch_voice / request_leave / shutdown — each documented in the module-level lifecycle table. Liveness derived from task state, not flags. Disconnect/listen race resolved via asyncio.wait + explicit cancel in finally.

add.header: MCPDriverLLMService is a tight frame-level adapter
add.description: ~30-line process_frame consumes UserTurnTextFrame to a shared queue and passes all other frames through (notably LLMTextFrame from convo_speak). Single responsibility, well-documented frame table.

add.header: Daemon protocol implemented twice
add.description: mcp-server/daemon_bridge.py duplicates the wire format and path constants from shared/daemon_protocol.py instead of importing them. Drift risk for any future protocol change.

add.header: VoiceProfileSwitcher carries dead RTVI handlers
add.description: After ticket 2ed2 moved voice switching to REST+SSE driven from VoiceChannel, voice_switcher.py still keeps handle_message / _handle_set_voice_profile / switch_profile — parallel state machines that never run from production paths.

add.header: CLI<->daemon HTTP boilerplate repeated
add.description: talky_cli.py reimplements urllib request + HTTPError/URLError + JSON parse + error print across each subcommand that talks to :9090. Asks for a small _daemon_get/_daemon_post helper.

add.header: Targeted state-machine unit tests on VoiceChannel
add.description: The mcp-server/tests/ suite uses pytest + AsyncMock + monkeypatch to isolate the TTL, request_leave, and greeting state machines from the live pipeline, with docstrings that explicitly call out what is and isn't covered. This is good unit-test discipline on a narrow slice.

add.header: Stale tests from incomplete rename
add.description: server/tests/test_config.py imports `server.config.profile_manager`, but the module lives at shared/profile_manager.py and all 19 production importers use the shared path. The test file was orphaned by the rename and presumably is not run in CI.

add.header: Print-driven scripts named test_*.py
add.description: server/tests/test_backends.py, server/tests/test_moltis_connection.py, and mcp-server/test_mcp.py masquerade as tests but have no assertions — they are manual smoke scripts gated by __main__. They inflate the apparent test count and mask the real coverage gap.

add.header: Critical entry points untested
add.description: talky_cli.py (931 LOC), mcp-server server.py (846 LOC), the VoiceChannel pipeline build/switch paths, voice_daemon.py, and both LLM backends have zero direct tests. The covered modules are leaf state machines; the orchestration spine is uncovered.

add.header: No snapshot tests, no timing/ordering fragility
add.description: Where tests exist they use deterministic mocks and explicit awaits; there is no snapshot overuse and no flaky external-state coupling. The strategy problem is volume and target selection, not fragility.

add.header: Uniform MCP tool decorator/async shape
add.description: All 12 MCP tools in mcp-server/src/pipecat_mcp_server/server.py use `@mcp.tool() async def` with typed params and docstrings — call-site shape is predictable across the surface.

add.header: Channel class async/sync split tracks IO vs query
add.description: VoiceChannel exposes async methods for IO-driven ops (speak, listen, switch_*, attach, request_leave) and sync methods for inspection (is_live, status, profiles_info). The split is principled, not accidental.

add.header: MCP tool result envelopes drift across siblings
add.description: Three incompatible shapes coexist: `{'success': bool, ...}` (local audio), `{'status': 'ok', 'active': name}` vs `{'error': str}` (switch_*), and raw `bool` (convo_speak). Callers cannot use a single predicate to detect failure, which is the dimension's primary friction.

add.header: Two transports for speak/listen are intentional, not duplication
add.description: `*_local_audio` tools target the unix-socket voice daemon; `convo_*` tools target the in-process browser pipeline. Distinct verbs, distinct preconditions, individually documented — coherent design choice rather than API drift.

add.header: Comments cite ticket IDs to anchor WHY
add.description: Most non-trivial blocks reference a ticket short-hash (e.g. 58db, 0b80, b3c4, 76a3) with one-line rationale. This keeps comments load-bearing — they're decisions, not narration of the next line.

add.header: Broad excepts marked with noqa:BLE001
add.description: `except Exception` blocks consistently carry `# noqa: BLE001`, signalling the suppression is intentional and flagged for the linter rather than accidental defensive overcatch.

add.header: WS backend template clone in moltis/openclaw
add.description: moltis.py and openclaw.py independently reimplement the same websocket lifecycle (connect, message handler, response queue, _next_id, _process_user_text). The shared structure is acknowledged in the moltis header but never extracted, so changes have to land twice.

add.header: Emoji-heavy backend logs
add.description: server/backends/* uses emoji prefixes on info/debug logs (🔌 ✅ ❌ 🤖 🗣️ 📨) ~27 times. Stylistic, not harmful, but a recognisable LLM-output signature in an otherwise restrained codebase.

add.header: Migrations are ticket-traced and documented in code
add.description: Module docstrings name the migration ticket (76a3, c3a1, 0c5d, 73b9) and explain the before/after frame shape. This makes it easy to understand which code path is the 'new' one, even when both still live in the tree.

add.header: TypeScript-only frontend, no JS holdovers
add.description: client/src is entirely .ts/.tsx with no .js/.jsx files alongside. No class/functional component coexistence. Frontend has none of the typical incomplete-TS-migration smells.

add.header: Stale compat shims accumulate without cleanup
add.description: server/tts_daemon.py, the module-level profile_manager singleton, and legacy_daemon_is_running each carry a 'backward compat' label but have no remaining callers. Compat code is added with discipline but not retired when callers move on.

add.header: Voice daemon unfinished half of aggregator migration
add.description: The MCP/talky daemon switched to TalkyUserTurnDetector + UserTurnTextFrame (ticket 76a3) but server/voice_daemon.py kept the older LLMContextAggregatorPair pattern. Both patterns coexist for the same operation, with the deferral acknowledged but no scheduled cutover.

add.header: Legacy labels do not always match active usage
add.description: /api/profile is labeled 'legacy compat for old CLI' but the in-tree CLI uses it exclusively; /api/profiles/switch was added as 'new' but has no in-tree caller. The compat-vs-canonical labels disagree with what the code actually does.

add.header: shared/ is a real utility hub, not a dumping ground
add.description: shared/ has avg_fan_in 6.8 and one-way edges from every consumer (server/, mcp-server/, root). The coupling matrix shows no reciprocal directory edges — the layering is a clean DAG, which is the property package_organization is supposed to protect.

add.header: Root entry points are justified, not stragglers
add.description: talky_cli.py is the declared `[project.scripts] talky` console entry, and talky_auth.py is its TUI sibling imported only by talky_cli and tests. Both belong at the repo root by convention, not by accident.

add.header: server/ root mixes three subsystems flat
add.description: STT (transcribe.py), legacy TTS daemon (tts_*), and current voice daemon (voice_*) coexist at one level alongside say_command.py and logging_config.py. A developer landing in server/ has to read each file to learn which subsystem it belongs to.

add.header: server/features/ is a one-occupant folder
add.description: Only voice_switcher.py lives under server/features/. The folder anticipates a grouping that does not yet exist, so it adds a navigation step without payoff.

add.header: Lazy getter pattern with eager module-level shadow
add.description: shared/profile_manager.py exposes a real `get_profile_manager()` getter that callers use consistently, but also assigns `profile_manager = get_profile_manager()` at module scope for legacy compat. The eager assignment performs filesystem I/O and YAML parsing at import time and has no remaining consumers — a concrete instance of the rubric's 'stale snapshot alongside dynamic getter' anti-pattern.

add.header: Endemic sys.path.insert at module scope
add.description: Seven non-test modules (CLI, voice daemon, TTS daemon/client, mcp-server channel, services_factory, say_command) mutate sys.path at import time to compensate for the project not being structured as a single installable package. This couples every entry point to a specific repo layout and produces duplicate-module hazards (e.g. `shared.service_factory` vs `service_factory`).

add.header: Lifespan-based warmup in VoiceChannel
add.description: channel.py defers heavy pipeline construction to the Starlette lifespan startup hook and per-connection callbacks rather than module import, with explicit reasoning documented in the module docstring. This is the right pattern — the issue is that server.py undercuts it by constructing the VoiceChannel and reading config eagerly at module scope.

add.header: Dead create_services_from_profile() with broken import
add.description: mcp-server/services_factory.py contains an unused factory whose import path (`config.profile_manager`) does not resolve in the current tree. It would ImportError at first call. Indicates init code paths that aren't exercised, raising risk that other init paths drift unnoticed.

add.header: Thin MCP tool layer over VoiceChannel
add.description: server.py exposes each MCP tool as a one-line delegation to voice_channel — the FastMCP layer carries no business logic, which keeps tool surface and channel implementation independently reviewable.

add.header: Cohesive small infrastructure modules
add.description: event_bus.py (64 LOC) and daemon_bridge.py (190 LOC) each handle one concern with no concern bleed — pub/sub broadcast and unix-socket RPC respectively. They demonstrate the codebase's preferred shape when domain complexity is bounded.

add.header: Profile-loading merge logic duplicates the constructor block
add.description: shared/profile_manager.py rebuilds LLMBackend / voice-backend dicts in three-to-four parallel branches across user-override / no-extensions / FileNotFound / broad-except paths. A two-stage merge-then-materialise pattern would collapse this and is the single highest-value design refactor available.

add.header: Pipeline build orchestration lives in one long function
add.description: VoiceChannel._build_and_start_pipeline coordinates LLM switcher, transport, voice switcher, turn detector, lifecycle handlers, pong responder, and runner kickoff in one ~200-line body. Cohesive in intent but mixed in concern — splitting into named helpers would improve traceability without adding indirection.

add.header: VoiceChannel methods document raise conditions
add.description: channel.py methods like listen(), speak(), and request_leave() declare return types and explicitly document RuntimeError on disconnect/no-pipeline plus edge-case semantics (grace window, MCP-mode wait). A developer can trust the signatures.

add.header: BaseLLMBackend service property fails loud
add.description: The service / input_service / output_service properties raise RuntimeError with the backend class name when accessed pre-init, instead of returning None — uninitialized access is impossible to confuse with success.

add.header: Optional default under non-optional annotation cluster
add.description: server/backends/openclaw.py repeatedly uses `param: str = None` instead of Optional[str] on token-loading and constructor surfaces. Pure annotation drift — runtime behavior is correct but type checkers and IDEs see a lie.

add.header: Sync shutdown helpers that don't actually wait
add.description: shared/service_factory.close_http_sessions() returns before close completes when an event loop is running, while its docstring presents it as a shutdown-time call. Documentation/behavior split per call context.

add.header: PiLLMService stub return type lie
add.description: send_message is annotated `-> str` but always raises NotImplementedError, and the class is named ...LLMService without subclassing pipecat's LLMService. Both name and signature overpromise relative to behavior.

add.header: Direct control flow across production tree
add.description: AST sweeps across server/, shared/, mcp-server/, and root CLI files surfaced zero identical-branch, dead-code, constant-condition, or `if x: return True/else: return False` smells. Branches are deliberate and exception-handling chains map distinct error classes to distinct outcomes.

add.header: Async-by-decoration in client_launcher seam
add.description: shared/client_launcher.py and one openclaw helper declare methods async even when bodies use only blocking subprocess/sleep or sync queue ops. Most other async functions are framework-mandated (FastMCP tools, Starlette routes, Pipecat event handlers), so the launcher cluster stands out as a localized concurrency fib.

add.header: type:ignore comments are scoped and explained
add.description: All 12 `type: ignore` comments live in test files (test_request_leave.py, test_channel_ttl.py) and carry specific error codes ([method-assign], [attr-defined]) for AsyncMock assignment patterns. No blanket ignores in production code.

add.header: Honest dataclasses for config domain objects
add.description: LLMBackend, VoiceProfile, TalkyProfile, and Event are dataclasses with Optional defaults marked deliberately, distinguishing required identity fields (name) from optional config (greeting, signoff, system_message). The shape of config data is visible at a glance.

add.header: dict[str, Any] used only where dynamic dispatch is real
add.description: The handful of dict[str, Any] sites (channel.py profile→LLMService map, event_bus.py SSE payloads) reflect genuinely polymorphic data rather than missed TypedDict opportunities. No premature flattening of structured types into Any-bags.

add.header: No parallel Literal/enum drift
add.description: The codebase does not define Enum classes redundantly with Literal aliases, and the one constant identifier (VoiceChannel.MCP_DRIVER_PROFILE = '__mcp__') is referenced by attribute everywhere rather than by raw string literal.

add.header: Optional flow-through in profile resolution
add.description: TalkyProfile fields llm_backend / voice_profile are typed Optional[str], but resolve_talky_profile and several internal call sites pass them straight into accessors typed `name: str`. The signatures are dishonest about None handling — error surfaces only via dict.get returning None.
